sources.short: EH 1909; Online vital records; Yale biographical sketches

cross_connections.display_label: her father-in-law, the great Yale geologist

narrative_blocks.header: Joined Bristol and Dana Yale academic dynasties
narrative_blocks.body: Caroline's October 1883 marriage joined the Bristols — her father William Brooks Bristol was Yale class of 1825 and three of her brothers were also Yale men — to the rising Dana scientific dynasty. Her husband Edward had been appointed Yale professor of physics four years earlier and would edit the American Journal of Science for the next fifty-one years. Her son James Dwight Dana would graduate Yale Phi Beta Kappa in 1911.

narrative_blocks.header: Brother-in-law's scrapbooks underpinned $35M New Haven bequest
narrative_blocks.body: Caroline's brother-in-law Arnold Guyot Dana compiled the 150-volume New Haven Old and New scrapbooks now held at the New Haven Museum — the definitive nineteenth-century record of the city. Arnold's grandson James Dana English indexed the entire collection during his lifetime and bequeathed thirty-five million dollars to New Haven nonprofits on his 2021 death. The scholarly legacy ran for four generations of the Dana family.

sources.short: EH 1909; Online vital records; Yale biographical sketches

cross_connections.display_label: her father-in-law, the great Yale geologist

Her husband Edward had been appointed Yale professor of physics four years earlier and would edit the American Journal of Science for the next fifty-one years. Her son James Dwight Dana would graduate Yale Phi Beta Kappa in 1911.

narrative_blocks.body: Caroline's brother-in-law Arnold Guyot Dana compiled the 150-volume New Haven Old and New scrapbooks now held at the New Haven Museum — the definitive nineteenth-century record of the city. notable.notable_blurb: Foremost American mineralogist of his era; Yale physics

narrative_blocks.header: Four generations edited American Journal of Science
narrative_blocks.detail: Dana's greatest contribution was the 1892 sixth edition of his father's System of Mineralogy — 1,134 pages, practically the work of one man, ten years in preparation. European mineralogists kept it on their desks; the Geological Society memorial records that Waldemar Schaller wore out three copies in thirty years on the U.S. Geological Survey. 'The Bible of every mineralogical institute I have ever been in,' wrote Charles Palache. Dana refused honorary degrees even from Yale. He died at 85, having edited the American Journal of Science for 51 years. (Geological Society memorial 1936; Wikipedia)
narrative_blocks.body: Edward Salisbury Dana was the fourth in an unbroken scientific line: Benjamin Silliman founded the American Journal of Science in 1818; his son Benjamin Jr. joined him; his son-in-law James Dwight Dana joined in 1846; and Edward joined in 1875, editing it until 1926. Both the Silliman and Trumbull families traced descent from John Alden and Priscilla Mullins.

narrative_blocks.header: Founded American petrology, taught Yale physics instead
narrative_blocks.detail: After Yale (1870, Scroll and Key) and two years under George Brush at Sheffield, he studied in Heidelberg and Vienna, then returned to Yale for his M.A. and Ph.D. He was elected to the National Academy of Sciences in 1884, the American Academy of Arts and Sciences in 1893, and the American Philosophical Society in 1896, along with scientific societies in Austria, Mexico, Russia, England, and Scotland. In 1925 the Mineralogical Society of America elected him Honorary President for life. His colleagues called him 'Eddie' or 'Ned' — known for unfailing good humor, bicycle rides into his eighties, and refusing to wear an overcoat. (Geological Society memorial 1936; Wikipedia)
narrative_blocks.body: Dana's 1872 paper on Connecticut Valley trap rocks founded the science of petrology in America. Yet because Yale had no mineralogy position available, he spent his teaching career as professor of physics — making the 1892 System of Mineralogy the work of a man who taught another subject entirely.

cross_connections.display_label: the geologist for whom the mineral pumpellyite is named

EH 1909; Online vital records; Yale biographical sketches

cross_connections.display_label: Yale University senior counsel; founded Bristol Stoddard Bristol 1888
cross_connections.notes: Uncle via mother HD2668 Caroline Bristol's brother. Founded Bristol Stoddard & Bristol 1888 (renamed Bristol Stoddard Beach & Fisher 1902, merged Bristol & White 1913). Yale University's senior counsel for many years; Yale Corporation passed memorial resolutions after his death.

cross_connections.display_label: Yale 1903 attorney, Bristol family law firm colleague
cross_connections.notes: Hooker-line cousins, both attorneys in the Bristol family law firm. HD2670 Yale 1911; HD04880 Yale 1903/Yale Law 1906, member of Bristol & White.

naming_inspiration.description: Named after his paternal grandfather James Dwight Dana (1813-1895), the pre-eminent American geologist of the nineteenth century who held Yale's Silliman Professorship from 1850 until 1892. As the first Hooker-line Dana man to bear the name, he revived the tradition that would later be continued by his nephew James Dwight 'Jim' Dana (1932-2007).

narrative_blocks.header: Joined Bristol and White, his uncles' law firm
narrative_blocks.body: Jim broke with the scientific tradition and pursued law, becoming a member of the Bristol and White law firm in New Haven — his maternal uncles' practice. He graduated Yale in 1911 with Phi Beta Kappa, the Second Barge Mathematical Prize, and a Philosophical Oration appointment. He joined his father's senior society Scroll and Key, continuing the Dana presence in that society into a second generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale class years stretched across three generations
narrative_blocks.body: Jim's Yale class lineage on his obituary spanned almost ninety years: grandfathers James Dwight Dana of 1833 and William Brooks Bristol of 1825, father Edward Salisbury Dana of 1870, and four uncles spread across Yale classes 1859 through 1883. Jim himself was Class of 1911 — designated 'J. Dwight Dana 11' to distinguish him from his namesake grandfather.

sources.short: EH 1909; Online vital records; Yale biographical sketches

cross_connections.display_label: Yale University senior counsel; founded Bristol Stoddard Bristol 1888
cross_connections.notes: Uncle via mother HD2668 Caroline Bristol's brother. Founded Bristol Stoddard & Bristol 1888. Yale University's senior counsel for many years.

cross_connections.display_label: Yale 1903 attorney, Bristol family law firm colleague
cross_connections.notes: Hooker-line cousins, both attorneys in the Bristol family law firm. HD2671 Yale 1918; HD04880 Yale 1903/Yale Law 1906.

narrative_blocks.header: Born Seal Harbor Maine, died Hartford at forty-nine
narrative_blocks.body: William 'Dish' Dana was born August 2, 1896 at the Dana family summer home at Seal Harbor on Mount Desert Island Maine. He died on August 23, 1945 in West Hartford Connecticut — twenty-one days after his forty-ninth birthday. His sons Ted and Jim, then teenagers, would both attend Yale and serve in the Korean War.

narrative_blocks.header: WWI draft registrant, Yale '18, Hartford banker
narrative_blocks.body: Dish registered for military service in 1917 during World War I while still a Yale undergraduate, and graduated Yale in 1918. He worked in banking in Hartford Connecticut and married Eugenia Mattie Hotchkiss in New Haven on September 24, 1927. His nickname 'Dish' appears in family records but its origin is undocumented.

naming_inspiration.description: Named after his paternal grandfather Edward Salisbury Dana (1849-1935), the foremost American mineralogist of his era and Silliman Professor at Yale. Ted himself attended Yale and earned a degree in Electrical Engineering, continuing the family's Yale scientific tradition.

military_service.notes: Lieutenant U.S. Air Force during the Korean War. Flew F-84 fighter-bombers in combat. Earned the DISTINGUISHED FLYING CROSS and the AIR MEDAL for combat distinction. Specific squadron and tour dates undocumented in available sources.

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT — private family service. Same cemetery as his grandfather X00898 Edward Salisbury Dana (1849-1935) and great-grandfather X00899 James Dwight Dana (1813-1895), the Yale scientific dynasty.

education.notes: Kingswood School (later Kingswood Oxford), West Hartford CT.
Hotchkiss School, Lakeville CT — boarding school. The Hotchkiss surname matches his mother M. Eugenia Hotchkiss — likely a family namesake school.
Yale University, B.A. in Electrical Engineering. Member of the Yale diving team.

career.notes: Followed his father William Bristol Dana into banking, working as a trust officer at Hartford National Bank.
Worked as an investment manager for many years after banking career.
Later career pivot: taught science at Westledge School in Simsbury CT.

narrative_blocks.header: Korean War USAF pilot, DFC and Air Medal
narrative_blocks.body: Edward 'Ted' Dana served as a Lieutenant in the U.S. Air Force during the Korean War flying F-84 fighter-bombers in combat. He earned the Distinguished Flying Cross and the Air Medal for combat distinction. He had just graduated Yale with a degree in Electrical Engineering when he entered the service.

narrative_blocks.header: Named after grandfather, the Yale mineralogist
narrative_blocks.body: Ted was named after his paternal grandfather Edward Salisbury Dana, the foremost American mineralogist of his era and Silliman Professor at Yale. Ted himself attended Yale and earned a degree in Electrical Engineering, continuing the family's Yale scientific tradition into the fourth generation since the legendary geologist James Dwight Dana.

narrative_blocks.header: Banker turned teacher, sailed Danamite into his eighties
narrative_blocks.body: Ted followed his father into banking as a trust officer at Hartford National Bank before pivoting late in life to teach science at Westledge School in Simsbury. He sailed his boat Danamite around Long Island Sound to Block Island and Nantucket, kept a family home named RoseAcre in Alstead New Hampshire, and continued to ski and walk and jog into his eighties.

naming_inspiration.description: Named after his great-grandfather James Dwight Dana (1813-1895), the pre-eminent American geologist of the 19th century, Copley Medal recipient, and Silliman Professor at Yale. This made Jim the third generation of Hooker-descendant 'James Dwight Dana' after his uncle HD2670 (1889-1951).

Also named after his paternal uncle James Dwight Dana (1889-1951), who himself had been named after their grandfather/great-grandfather, the famous Yale geologist. By Jim's 1932 birth the name had passed down three generations.

military_service.notes: U.S. Army during the Korean Conflict — took a two-year leave from his Yale studies to serve. Specific unit, rank, and dates of service undocumented. Returned to Yale and graduated 1954.

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT (per obit stated intent). Same cemetery as his great-grandfather X00899 James Dwight Dana (1813-1895), his grandfather X00898 Edward Salisbury Dana, his uncle HD2670 James Dwight Dana (1889-1951), and his older brother Edward Salisbury 'Ted' Dana.

education.notes: Phillips Academy Andover, class of 1950 — different prep school path than his brother Ted (Kingswood + Hotchkiss).
Yale University, B.A. 1954. Took two-year leave from Yale to serve in the U.S. Army during the Korean Conflict.

career.role: John R. Kimberly Distinguished Professor of Economics
career.notes: Entire academic career at Lawrence University. Held the John R. Kimberly Distinguished Professorship of Economics. Chair of Economics Department for twenty-one years; Interim Dean of the Faculty. Won the 1978 Lawrence Excellence in Teaching Award. Taught Microeconomics, Corporate Finance, International Economics. Taught at Lawrence's London Center in 1972 and 1983. Officially retired 2001 but continued teaching until earlier in 2007, the year of his death.
Also taught courses at the University of Wisconsin–Madison.
Served as expert witness in trials involving the determination of economic loss.

narrative_blocks.header: Two-year leave from Yale for Korean Army service
narrative_blocks.body: James 'Jim' Dana took a two-year leave from his Yale studies to serve in the US Army during the Korean Conflict, returning to graduate with his Yale BA in 1954. He went on to earn a doctorate in Economics from MIT. His older brother Edward had served as an Air Force lieutenant flying F-84 fighter-bombers in the same war.

narrative_blocks.header: Lawrence University John Kimberly Distinguished Professor
narrative_blocks.body: Jim spent his entire academic career at Lawrence University in Appleton Wisconsin as the John R. Kimberly Distinguished Professor of Economics. He chaired the Economics Department for twenty-one years and served as Interim Dean of the Faculty.

narrative_blocks.header: Third generation James Dwight Dana, all Yale men
narrative_blocks.body: Jim was the third generation of Hooker-descendant James Dwight Danas — named after his great-grandfather James Dwight Dana (1813-1895), the pre-eminent American geologist and Silliman Professor at Yale, as well as his uncle James Dwight Dana (1889-1951). All three were Yale men. Jim and his brother Edward also both named their sailboats Danamite — Jim's sailed on Lake Winnebago, Edward's on Long Island Sound.

sources.short: EH 1909; Online vital records; Yale biographical sketches

notable.notable_blurb: New Haven mayor and federal judge

narrative_blocks.header: He rose from Speaker to federal judge
narrative_blocks.body: William Bristol served as Speaker of the Connecticut House, mayor of New Haven, and a state appellate judge before John Quincy Adams named him to the federal bench in 1826. He had declined a nomination for governor the year before.

narrative_blocks.header: He took his father-in-law's seat on the bench
narrative_blocks.body: Bristol's federal judgeship had been vacated by his own father-in-law, Pierpont Edwards. He had married Edwards's daughter Sarah in 1805.
